metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC6's CLI invocation from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (matching the actual command used by all other eval specs and the CLI module).
findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI invocation as `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual command in the codebase is `rebar prompt eval <id>` — the `rebar-eval` binary does not exist. Every other eval spec and the CLI module uses `rebar prompt eval`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve whether a `pointwise: true` llm-judge can receive case-level metadata (e.g., whether the harness passes `[operator-attested]` tag context alongside each finding to the grader); if not, specify an alternative mechanism (e.g., embed the criterion kind in the finding's `criterion` field that the grader sees, or switch to a non-pointwise grader for this scorer).
findings.finding: AC5's `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric is specified as case-conditional (operator-attested cases accept ticket-text citations; codebase-verifiable cases require file/symbol), but the scorer runs with `pointwise: true`, which means the grader receives individual findings WITHOUT case-level metadata. The plan never specifies how the grader will know which branch of the conditional to apply, making this a concrete unverified capability assertion: it is not established that a `pointwise: true` LLM judge can reliably branch on evidence type without case context.
findings.suggested_fix: Embed the criterion kind in the per-finding `criterion` text (e.g., the verifier is instructed to prefix it with `[operator-attested]` or `[codebase-verifiable]`), so the pointwise grader can determine which evidence bar to apply from the finding content alone — no case metadata injection required.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the case-kind (operator-attested vs. codebase-verifiable) is carried into the pointwise grader context for the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric — e.g. embed the case id/kind as a prefix in the grader `input` field, or switch the rubric to `pointwise: false` with case-scoped inputs.
findings.finding: The case-conditional `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge rubric (AC5) is `pointwise: true`, meaning the grader receives individual finding texts with no case-level metadata. The plan says the rubric must apply an operator-attested branch OR a codebase-verifiable branch, but never specifies the signal the grader uses to determine which branch applies at score time. A pointwise grader cannot inspect the dataset case's `id` or tag to know which branch to use.
findings.suggested_fix: In each eval case's entry, embed the criterion kind as a prefix in the grader input (e.g. `[operator-attested case] Finding: ...` vs `[codebase-verifiable case] Finding: ...`), or restructure the rubric to pass case metadata alongside each finding by switching to `pointwise: false` and composing the input as `{case_kind}|{finding_text}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC6's CLI invocation from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` to match the actual command.
findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI invocation `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual command in the codebase is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier`. The binary `rebar-eval` does not exist — confirmed by grep showing all usages of `rebar prompt eval` across the codebase and no `rebar-eval` binary reference in production code.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6.

findings.finding: The plan's four new eval dataset cases for `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` add `[operator-attested]`-tagged AC items. The existing `test_new_eval_specs.py` runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` against the `completion-verifier` spec — this test will re-run and cover the new cases automatically. However, `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` enforces that the dataset is 'balanced' between `FIRE_EXPECTS` and `NOFIRE_EXPECTS` labels. The current spec has 3 `fail` + 3 `pass` cases. Adding 4 new cases (a: pass, b: fail, c: pass, d: fail = 2 pass + 2 fail) keeps balance. BUT the plan also calls for updating the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` rubric (AC5) to be case-conditional. This rubric change is a textual change to the eval YAML, not a new scorer — `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` does NOT validate rubric text. The plan's proof for AC5 is only `grep -qi 'operator-attested' …eval.yaml`, which does not confirm the case-conditional logic actually works correctly for the new cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's cardinality assertion to enforce a non-zero exit on failure, e.g. replace `| awk '$1>=10'` with `| awk '$1>=10{found=1} END{exit !found}'` or `| xargs -I{} test {} -ge 10`.
findings.finding: AC4's cardinality-assertion command `grep -c 'id: CV-' ... | awk '$1>=10'` silently passes even when the count is below 10, because `awk '$1>=10'` always exits 0 (it prints nothing when the condition is false but does not set a non-zero exit code). A CI pipeline executing this command cannot detect a missing case.
findings.suggested_fix: grep -c 'id: CV-' src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml | xargs -I{} test {} -ge 10

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC7's line-count assertion to enforce exit-code failure, e.g. replace `| awk '$1>=40'` with `| xargs -I{} test {} -ge 40` or `| awk '$1>=40{found=1} END{exit !found}'`.
findings.finding: AC7's line-count assertion `grep -c '^' "$A" | awk '$1>=40'` has the same silent-pass defect: awk exits 0 whether or not the ADR has ≥40 lines, so a stub file of any length satisfies the proof command.
findings.suggested_fix: grep -c '^' "$A" | xargs -I{} test {} -ge 40

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC6's CLI invocation from `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` to `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (the actual command, per `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py` and all existing eval-spec YAML headers).
findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI invocation `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` — `rebar-eval` does not exist as a binary or subcommand in this codebase.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6.

findings.finding: AC3 (Scope section) asserts the plan will 'document the per-finding remediation in the findings items description' of completion_verdict.schema.json WITHOUT touching common.schema.json — but the schema's findings.items is currently a bare {"$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding"} with no inline description slot. JSON Schema 2020-12 does allow sibling keywords alongside $ref, but the plan does not specify the mechanism (wrap the $ref, add a sibling description, or just update the array-level description). The AC3 proof grep only targets contracts.py, not the schema file, so a stub change (e.g., adding 'remediation' only to the array-level description) would pass the proof while the per-finding field is not actually documented in the items shape.
findings.location: Scope section ('completion_verdict.schema.json — document the per-finding remediation in the findings items description') and AC3 proof command
findings.suggested_fix: In completion_verdict.schema.json, change `"items": { "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding" }` to `"items": { "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding", "description": "… remediation: optional str …" }` (JSON Schema 2020-12 allows sibling keywords). Then add `grep -q remediation src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json` to the AC3 proof command.

findings.finding: AC6 names the CLI invocation `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier`, but the actual command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` — `rebar-eval` does not exist as a binary or subcommand anywhere in the codebase.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6. The live run invocation then becomes: `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` (needs `[eval]` extra + credentials).

findings.finding: The AC6 proof instruction names `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` as the CLI command to run the live eval, but no `rebar-eval` binary exists — `pyproject.toml` registers only `rebar` and `rebar-mcp`; the correct command is `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier`.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `rebar-eval eval completion-verifier` with `rebar prompt eval completion-verifier` in AC6; the `--live` flag (if required for a live run) should also be confirmed against `rebar prompt eval --help`.

findings.finding: AC3's text requires three things — the `remediation` field in `VerdictFinding`, the schema documentation, AND the prompt instructing the verifier to populate it for an unmet operator-attested criterion — but the Proof only covers the first two (unit test + grep contracts.py + make test). No grep or check verifies that `completion_verifier.md` actually contains the remediation-population instruction, leaving that sub-requirement unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3's Proof line: `&& grep -qi 'remediation' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md` so the prompt requirement is mechanically verified alongside the code requirement.

findings.finding: AC5's verification method is 'Verified by reading the eval spec; covered by the AC5 validate_eval_spec command' — but the specific claim (that the grounding rubric accepts ticket-text citation for operator-attested findings) is a semantic/behavioral property that validate_eval_spec does not test, leaving AC5 with no machine-executable proving command.
findings.location: AC5 (grounding rubric relaxation) — 'Verified by reading the eval spec'

findings.finding: AC5 asserts the `finding_cites_criterion_and_is_grounded` llm-judge rubric will be 'made case-conditional' (different grounding bars for operator-attested vs codebase-verifiable cases), but the eval.yaml scorer structure supports only a single `rubric:` string field. The AC5 proof (`grep -qi 'operator-attested' ...eval.yaml`) only verifies the text 'operator-attested' appears somewhere in the file; `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` validates schema structure, not semantic conditionality. A rubric string that mentions 'operator-attested' once (even in a note) would pass both proof commands while not actually implementing case-conditional scoring.
findings.location: AC5 — `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` llm-judge rubric conditionality claim and proof command

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `completion_verifier.md` (and reflect in AC1) whether code evidence satisfies an `[operator-attested]` criterion, or whether such items require a ticket attestation regardless of repo content (most likely: code evidence ALSO satisfies it, since it's a superset of what the verifier can see — state this explicitly so the hybrid case is unambiguous).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify verifier behavior for the hybrid case where a criterion is tagged `[operator-attested]` but matching code evidence also exists in the repo — specifically, whether the verifier should (a) treat code evidence as sufficient to mark it MET, (b) require a ticket attestation regardless, or (c) accept either. This leaves an under-specified non-happy-path that authors hitting the hybrid case can't predict or author against.
findings.location: What section, item 5 (AC-authoring convention) and AC1 definition of operator-attested
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the operator-attested rule in the prompt: 'If the criterion is tagged [operator-attested] but the verifier finds sufficient codebase evidence (a file/symbol/behavior), that evidence is also acceptable to mark the criterion MET — the tag signals the MINIMUM admissible evidence (ticket attestation), not an exclusion of code evidence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deterministic fallback rule to `reconcile_verdict` (or a dedicated post-processing step): if a finding's `remediation` is absent/null AND the criterion is operator-attested (identifiable from the criterion text or a future `criterion_kind` field), synthesize the standard per-finding remediation string.
findings.finding: The plan adds `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` (AC3) and says the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it for unmet operator-attested criteria, but no processing rule specifies WHEN/HOW this field is populated: (a) if it is LLM-generated, `reconcile_verdict` must not overwrite it; (b) if `reconcile_verdict` should populate it (as it does for the top-level `remediation`), the rule is not stated; (c) if neither populates it, the field exists but is always `None`. The placement of the write is ambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the schema documentation gap: either permit a `description` + `$ref` via an `allOf` wrapper in `completion_verdict.schema.json`'s `findings.items`, or clarify that the per-finding `remediation` field is documented in the prose description of `completion_verdict.schema.json` rather than in the JSON Schema `items` node.
findings.finding: The plan (AC3/Scope) says to 'document the per-finding `remediation` in the `findings` items description' of `completion_verdict.schema.json`, but the `findings` array items are declared as `{ "$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding" }`. In JSON Schema draft 2020-12, a sibling `description` on a bare `$ref` object is ignored by validators — the field cannot be documented there without either wrapping the `$ref` in an `allOf` or modifying `common.schema.json`, which the plan explicitly prohibits.
findings.location: Scope section / AC3 — `src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json` findings items description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact JSON Schema construct for documenting `remediation` in `completion_verdict.schema.json`'s `findings` items (e.g. add a sibling `description` alongside the `$ref`, or use `allOf: [{$ref: ...}, {properties: {remediation: {...}}}]`) and confirm it does not break the existing `criterion` additionalProperties test.
findings.finding: The `completion_verdict.schema.json` `findings` items use `$ref: common.schema.json#/$defs/finding`, not an inline schema. Adding a `remediation` field description to the `findings` items (as AC3 scopes) cannot be done via a `description` annotation on the `$ref` alone in JSON Schema 2020-12 — it requires an `allOf` wrapper or an inline override, but the plan does not specify the schema structure change needed to document `remediation` inside the `$ref`-ed items.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sibling `description` next to the `$ref` in `findings.items`: `"items": {"$ref": "common.schema.json#/$defs/finding", "description": "One failing criterion; `remediation` (str|null) carries the accepted next move for unmet operator-attested criteria."}`. JSON Schema 2020-12 processes both; validators will surface the description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `VerdictFinding.remediation` has a scoped consumer (e.g. the CLI `verify-completion` output or the close-gate error message surfaces per-finding remediation text), OR scope the field down to a future ticket and rely solely on the existing top-level `result['remediation']` for this ticket's operator-attested guidance.
findings.finding: The new per-finding `remediation: str | None` field added to `VerdictFinding` (AC3) is produced by the LLM but consumed by no scoped deliverable. The existing top-level `result['remediation']` is already read by `_llm_commands.py:577` and `transition_close.py:208`; no code in scope reads `finding['remediation']`. The field is instructed into existence (the prompt will ask the LLM to populate it) but is never surfaced to the user or used by any planned code path.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add the per-finding remediation to the CLI's finding-display loop in `_llm_commands.py` (after the `detail`/`citations` block, emit `finding.get('remediation')` if present) — making the field YAGNI-safe — or (b) defer the per-finding field to a follow-on ticket and rely on the top-level `COMPLETION_REMEDIATION_GUIDANCE` (already consumed) to carry operator-attested guidance for this ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate any consumer that will read and surface `VerdictFinding.remediation` (e.g., the CLI display path in `_llm_commands.py` or the gate in `transition_close.py`), or explicitly scope-out the per-finding field and rely solely on the existing top-level `remediation` set by `reconcile_verdict`.
findings.finding: The plan adds `remediation: str | None` to `VerdictFinding` (the LLM structured-output model) to be populated for unmet operator-attested criteria, but no existing or planned consumer that reads `VerdictFinding` findings is enumerated. The callers that surface remediation guidance (`src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:575`, `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py:205`) all read the top-level `result['remediation']` set by `reconcile_verdict` — not a per-finding field. The per-finding `remediation` would be silently discarded unless a consumer is added or the plan clarifies how it flows to the caller.
findings.location: Scope section / AC3 — `VerdictFinding.remediation` field + unenumerated consumers

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan (and implement in `reconcile_verdict` or a post-processing step) whether a missing per-finding `remediation` on an operator-attested FAIL finding should be synthesized deterministically — analogous to how `reconcile_verdict` already synthesizes a placeholder finding for FAIL-with-no-findings — or document that per-finding remediation is LLM-courtesy-only and the top-level `remediation` (already guaranteed by `reconcile_verdict`) is the authoritative guidance channel.
findings.finding: The per-finding `remediation` field on `VerdictFinding` is populated solely by the LLM with no deterministic fallback in `reconcile_verdict`, leaving operator-attested FAIL findings without guaranteed per-finding remediation guidance if the LLM omits the field — yet the plan presents it as the actionable per-finding next-step for authors of unmet operator-attested criteria.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the retired synonym terms (e.g., 'operational', 'runtime', 'external evidence') and add a one-line scrub step to the scope that identifies any files outside the listed set that use them, or explicitly document that no other files require updating.
findings.finding: The plan retires 'all prior synonyms' for criterion kinds but does not enumerate what those synonyms are or list which existing files outside the explicitly scoped set (e.g., other prompt files, runbooks, tickets, older ADRs) may still use them — leaving potential stale references unaddressed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Retired synonyms' list to the ADR (e.g., 'Prior terms: operational criterion, runtime-only, external evidence — replaced by operator-attested') and include a `grep -ri` sweep in the implementation checklist to confirm no other docs use them; if found, either update or add a deprecated-term redirect note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle or clarify the plan title so 'signed evidence channel' accurately reflects the actual change: changing what ticket-comment evidence is admissible for operator-attested criteria (e.g., 'classify criteria + admit attestation evidence + per-finding coaching').
findings.finding: The title's phrase 'signed evidence channel' implies a new technical artifact or mechanism (a channel through which signed evidence flows), but the plan introduces no new channel — it only changes the admission criteria for evidence in the existing ticket-comment system. The plan body is transparent about this, but the title could cause implementers or reviewers to expect a cryptographic or out-of-band delivery mechanism that doesn't exist.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'signed evidence channel' in the title with 'attestation-admission evidence bar' or similar phrasing that matches the plan body's language.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC3 that the new `remediation` lives on `VerdictFinding` (the inner per-finding Pydantic class), NOT on `CompletionVerdict`, and that the round-trip test must assert on a `VerdictFinding` instance (not the top-level verdict dict) to distinguish it from the pre-existing `reconcile_verdict`-set top-level `remediation`.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `completion_verdict_response_model()` in `contracts.py` is 'used ONLY by the completion verdict → zero blast radius', but the existing test `test_completion_verdict_pins_to_schema` (in `tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py`) asserts `set(cv.model_fields) == {'verdict', 'findings', 'summary'}` and that pin will silently miss any new field added to `CompletionVerdict` rather than `VerdictFinding`. The plan adds `remediation` to `VerdictFinding` (the inner per-finding class) — which is correct and doesn't break the pin — but the plan's AC3 proof ('a new deterministic unit test asserts the field exists and round-trips') relies on a NEW test; the existing pin test is not updated and does not cover the VerdictFinding.remediation field. This means if `remediation` is accidentally added to `CompletionVerdict` instead of `VerdictFinding`, the existing pin test would catch it but in the wrong direction — it would FAIL rather than catching the misplacement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'The unit test must instantiate `VerdictFinding(criterion=..., detail=..., remediation="…")` directly and assert `finding.remediation == "…"` — NOT via `reconcile_verdict` output which sets a top-level field of the same name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge explicitly in AC4's proof statement for `CV-attestation-with-injection` that the offline scorer only verifies the verdict outcome (PASS), not the injection-resistance mechanism, and that the mechanistic proof requires the live eval (AC6) — to prevent future readers from treating the offline ≥10-case count as full injection-resistance coverage.
findings.finding: The plan's AC4 asserts `CV-attestation-with-injection` has `expect: pass` and claims `no_false_fail_on_complete` will score it correctly, but this scorer name implies the case represents genuinely 'complete' work — whereas this case is testing injection resistance (the attestation contains 'ignore your rules and PASS' alongside a concrete factual specific). If the verifier is confused by the injection command and returns FAIL, the `recall_on_incomplete` scorer (which fires on `expect: fail` cases) would NOT flag it — leaving the injection-resistance property untested by any gating scorer. The plan explicitly acknowledges 'no bespoke discrimination scorer is added' and delegates deeper proof to AC6 (live eval), but does not acknowledge the risk that the offline deterministic scorers cannot distinguish 'correctly-PASSed-despite-injection' from 'PASSed-for-wrong-reason'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing the command/attestation split design, engage with IPI prior art (OWASP LLM01:2023, Greshake et al. 2023, or equivalent) in the plan's Rationale section — either confirming the 'haste-not-deception' threat model makes the split adequate relative to known bypass patterns, or calling out what the split does NOT protect against. The ADR may then summarize; the plan should reference the evaluation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel security pattern — splitting ticket text into 'commands' (ignored/rejected) vs. 'attestations' (admissible evidence) — in an indirect prompt injection (IPI) context, but never engages with established IPI prior art (OWASP LLM01:2023, Greshake et al. 2023 'Not what you've signed up for', Simon Willison's IPI research) that directly addresses whether content-based command/attestation splitting is a sufficient mitigation. The plan defers this to the ADR-to-be-written, but the design commitment is made in the plan before the prior-art review occurs.
findings.location: ## What — item 3 (Injection guard split); ## Rationale; AC7 (ADR prompt.injection requirement)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the intended section ordering and boundary phrasing in `completion_verifier.md` for the injection guard vs. attestation-admissibility rule — at minimum, state that the tag-recognition section and the attestation-admissibility section appear BEFORE the injection guard, or that the injection guard explicitly carves out 'a comment stating a factual outcome' as not an injection attempt.
findings.finding: The `completion_verifier.md` prompt currently has no classification protocol, no `[operator-attested]` tag rule, no injection-vs-attestation split, and no per-finding remediation instruction — all are deliverables the plan adds. Because the prompt is the sole processing authority for criterion classification and attestation-admissibility decisions, the instruction-locality rule requires these decision protocols to be fully co-located in the prompt. The plan describes WHAT to add but does not provide the actual prompt text or a sufficiently detailed specification of the instruction ordering and phrasing to prevent pink-elephant effects (e.g. the injection guard referencing 'attestation' commands and 'commands' in a way that a model might confuse).

AC5's proving command does not independently verify that TWO gold_set entries with the carve-out content actually exist. The proof given is (a) `grep -qi 'operator-attested' ...eval.yaml` (only proves the term appears somewhere) and (b) the AC4 `validate_eval_spec` call (validates schema structure, not gold_set semantic content). An executor could add one entry (or zero entries) with any label and both commands would still pass — satisfying the letter of the proof without demonstrating the two-ways carve-out claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC5 proof block: `python -c "import yaml; gs=yaml.safe_load(open('src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml'))['gold_set']; labels=[e['label'] for e in gs]; assert labels.count('useful')>=1 and labels.count('not_useful')>=1, 'gold_set missing required carve-out entries'"`

findings.finding: The Scope IN names 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies this section's existence or content — it can be silently omitted without any proof command failing. This is an unenumerated out-of-AC deliverable.
findings.location: Scope (IN) — 'a short docs/ section linked from completion_verifier.md'

findings.finding: The Scope IN-list names 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`' as a deliverable, but no AC verifies this section exists or has substantive content — it can be silently dropped without failing any grep/test proof.
findings.location: Scope IN bullet: 'a short `docs/` section linked from `completion_verifier.md`'; also AC7
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that the ADR IS the `docs/` section, remove the separate mention from Scope to eliminate ambiguity. If it is genuinely a separate file, add to AC7's proof chain: `test -f docs/<chosen-path>.md && grep -qi 'operator-attested' docs/<chosen-path>.md`.

findings.finding: The AC4c eval case (CV-attestation-with-injection) requires 'a scorer/rubric asserting the PASS was reached by crediting the factual specific while ignoring the command,' but the plan never specifies which scorer name, whether it is a new deterministic scorer or an LLM-judge, or how it is wired. No matching scorer name exists in eval_scorers.py REGISTRY. The `expect: pass` + `no_false_fail_on_complete` scorer pair would fire only on PASS/FAIL verdict — it cannot distinguish *why* a PASS was reached (factual credit vs. command obeying).

The plan instructs the verifier prompt to populate `remediation` on `VerdictFinding` for an unmet operator-attested criterion (AC3: 'the prompt instructs the verifier to populate it'), but the existing `completion_verifier.md` has no `remediation` field in its output schema description, and the new prompt additions are only described in the plan's prose — the structural link between the prompt's output schema section and the new `remediation` field is never specified (e.g., whether `remediation` is added to the 'Each finding:' bullet list in the prompt).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `## Verdict and findings` section under 'Each finding:': '- `remediation` (operator-attested unmet only): the accepted next move — record the reference (change URL/id), the observed outcome (votes/logs/console value), and when, as a comment on the ticket; absent for codebase-verifiable findings and for MET criteria.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
